title: CD19 density floor of ~2,000 molecules/cell for CAR-T response in B-ALL

context.condition: CD19 CAR-T in B-ALL lymphoblasts; below this density, response is significantly less likely (Majzner et al., 2020)

schema_fit.constraint: The chapter states this as a floor/threshold (density *below* which response drops off), not a single measured data point. QuantityLeaf has no way to distinguish floor-vs-observation semantics -- value=2000 is stored exactly as a directly measured antigen count would be.
schema_fit.current_ir_behavior: Stored as a plain FUNDAMENTAL quantity (value=2000, unit='molecules/cell'), indistinguishable from a point measurement.
schema_fit.candidate_resolution: Add an optional `value_relation` enum on QuantityLeaf ('floor'|'ceiling'|'point'|'range') so floor/threshold semantics survive into the IR.
schema_fit.purity_cost: Additive and optional, defaulting to 'point' for byte-identity with all existing claims; touches only QuantityLeaf, not the core Claim/pattern grammar.

title: The CD3 complex contributes 10 ITAMs to TCR signaling
HLA-A*02:01 allele-level prevalence is ~27% in individuals of European descent

context.condition: rigorous allele-level estimate, European descent (Tano-Menka et al., 2023)

title: ImmTAC (tebentafusp) was engineered to ~1,000,000-fold higher pMHC affinity than a natural TCR
Synapse discrimination is a kinetic bond-lifetime measurement via kinetic proofreading, not an affinity measurement

leaf.data: Discrimination at the immune synapse is a measurement of bond lifetime (dwell time, tau = 1/k_off) processed through kinetic proofreading, not a measurement of equilibrium binding affinity (K_D).
leaf.warrant: Kinetic proofreading requires a bound ligand to survive a chain of slow, energy-consuming modification steps (TCR ITAM phosphorylation by Lck, ZAP-70 recruitment and its own multi-step activation) before the signal fires; because the probability of completing all steps scales supralinearly (near-exponentially) with dwell time, a small difference in tau produces a large difference in output, giving sharp self/non-self discrimination that a simple equilibrium K_D readout cannot provide.
leaf.rebuttal: At high ligand density and fast k_off, serial triggering can let a single pMHC sequentially engage and trigger many TCRs, amplifying signal from very few ligands by a route other than completed proofreading.

title: Increasing receptor affinity beyond the kinetic optimum degrades, not improves, antigen discrimination

leaf.data: Increasing TCR/CAR receptor affinity beyond an optimal dwell-time sweet spot does not improve, and actively degrades, antigen discrimination.
leaf.warrant: Pushing K_D low enough slows k_off so far that serial triggering collapses (each receptor stays occupied by one ligand it cannot release), the proofreading chain completes even for low-density/self engagements because bond lifetime now always outlasts the proofreading delay, and the sigmoidal density-response saturates and flattens -- erasing the density threshold that produced tumor/normal selectivity.
leaf.rebuttal: Soluble bridging reagents such as ImmTAC are exempt from this paradox because they do not rely on synapse-level kinetic proofreading for discrimination; discrimination is instead delegated to the specificity of the engineered binding interface and the requirement to cross-link CD3, so million-fold affinity gains remain beneficial for a soluble reagent even though they would be catastrophic for a cellular receptor.

title: A second-generation CAR hardwires Signal 1 and Signal 2 into one chain, deleting the natural Signal-2 checkpoint

leaf.data: A second-generation CAR fuses antigen recognition (Signal 1, via scFv) and costimulation (Signal 2, via a CD28 or 4-1BB domain) into a single polypeptide triggered by one binding event, deleting the independent Signal-2 checkpoint that a natural T cell uses to prevent autoreactive activation.
leaf.warrant: A natural T cell requires independent engagement of CD28 by CD80/CD86 on a professional antigen-presenting cell as a separate licensing signal; Signal 1 without Signal 2 drives anergy, a built-in safety interlock. Because the CAR fuses scFv -> hinge -> transmembrane -> costimulatory domain -> CD3zeta into one chain, a single binding event supplies both signals simultaneously, so the independent-context requirement (and the anergy interlock it enforces) is structurally removed.
leaf.rebuttal: synNotch-gated CAR-deployment schemes (synNotch(antigen A) -> CAR(antigen B)) partially restore a Signal-2-like contextual check by requiring a second, independent antigen-recognition event before the CAR is even expressed.

title: HLA restriction is a population-genetic ceiling on pMHC-directed therapies, not an engineering bug

leaf.data: HLA restriction is not an engineering bug to be optimized away; it is a thermodynamic and population-genetic ceiling on the entire surface-pMHC-directed therapeutic strategy.
leaf.warrant: Each TCR or TCR-mimic product is locked to one peptide displayed on one HLA allele; allele frequency varies sharply by ancestry (e.g., ~27% for HLA-A*02:01 in European descent, lower in African and many Asian populations), and even in an HLA-matched patient tumors can further evade by downregulating or deleting MHC-I (beta2-microglobulin mutation, HLA LOH) -- so no single allele-restricted product, however well engineered, can achieve population-wide coverage or evade acquired HLA loss.
leaf.rebuttal: Multi-allele product portfolios can raise aggregate population coverage without solving the ceiling for any single product, and the ceiling does not apply to non-HLA-restricted approaches such as native-surface-protein CARs, which read protein directly rather than through pMHC.
